edibility: Generally edible and highly prized for its meaty, chicken-like texture and flavor. Collect only young, brightly colored brackets with soft texture; mature specimens become tough and acidic. Requires thorough cooking. About 10% of people may experience allergic reactions ranging from mild (swollen lips) to severe (nausea, vomiting, dizziness). Never harvest from conifers or yew trees, as absorption of host toxins can be lethal.
taste: Tender young specimens have a savory, chicken-like flavor with a meaty texture. Mature specimens become increasingly tough, fibrous, and develop acidic notes. The palatability depends heavily on age and thorough preparation.
search: Found on dead or dying hardwoods (oak, maple, beech) in deciduous woodlands, urban parks, and forest edges across North America, Europe, and UK. Season: late summer through fall (August-November), occasionally as early as June. Look-alikes include jack-o'-lantern mushroom (which has gills and vase shape) and Dyer's polypore (brown bruising, velvety texture, found on conifers). True chicken of the woods has pores, fan/bracket shape, bright yellow-orange color.

toxicity.symptoms: Allergic reactions in ~10% of consumers: swollen lips, nausea, vomiting, dizziness, disorientation
toxicity.treatmentWindow: Monitor for symptoms; most reactions subside with supportive care
toxicity.antidote: Supportive care only. Ensure thorough cooking and proper identification. Those with mushroom allergies or sensitive digestive systems should avoid.
